_doc: Worked examples the book uses to ground abstract claims. Each case study illustrates one or more Mechanisms or Concepts and appears in specific Chapters.

caseStudies.label: The telephone game
caseStudies.summary: Ten kids pass a sentence around a circle; the output barely resembles the input. The canonical pure-transport intuition — but note that the game forces every player to pass, removing the selection step that real social networks have.

caseStudies.label: Hammer of the Witches vs. Copernicus
caseStudies.summary: Same press, same century. Hammer sold out; Copernicus was 'an all-time worst seller.' Pure-transport reads it as compressed-vs-complex. Selection reads it as identical transport cost with massively different fitness against contemporary criteria — fear, urgency, action affordance.

caseStudies.label: matplotlib vs. seaborn
caseStudies.summary: matplotlib: general, powerful, hard to use. seaborn: opinionated abstraction, lower ceiling, broader access. The 'software stratification' pattern — same trade-off shows up in religions, political platforms, scientific popularization.

caseStudies.label: Astrology
caseStudies.summary: Astronomical positions are real measurements; the interpretive framework is invented. Never survived consensus (never went through it), injected directly at curation and meme stages. Canonical example of manufactured content propagating through the same channels as measured content.

caseStudies.label: 'Scientists say X' headline
caseStudies.summary: A 20-year paper compresses to a meme within a week. Pure-transport says lossy hops; selection says the headline writer chose the highest-fitness finding, not the most load-bearing one. Both pipelines are operating.

caseStudies.label: Bitcoin: 'internet money' vs. full technical description
caseStudies.summary: 'Bitcoin is internet money' requires almost no preconditions. The full settlement-layer description requires five or six. Same object, two forms — only the compressed form has a chance of reaching unprepared receivers.

caseStudies.label: Power posing — the meme that survived its own corrective
caseStudies.summary: Carney/Cuddy/Yap 2010 reported that 'high-power' poses raised testosterone, lowered cortisol, and improved risk-task performance. Published in Psychological Science, popularized via Cuddy's 70M-view TED talk and the book Presence. Ranehill et al. 2015 failed to replicate; Carney publicly disavowed the work in 2016. Within the field the original is treated as a canonical replication-crisis case; in popular culture the meme keeps spreading, because the corrective signal has worse curation fitness than the original. Made-to-meme + meme-survived-the-correction is the chapter's prototype case.

caseStudies.label: Arsenic-life (Wolfe-Simon et al. 2010) — died at consensus
caseStudies.summary: December 2010 Science paper claiming GFAJ-1 bacteria could substitute arsenic for phosphorus in DNA. NASA press conference; initial curation began. Within hours independent biochemists pointed out methodological problems; within 18 months Science published technical comments and refutation showing the original measurement had been contaminated. Scientific consensus rejected the claim. The meme never propagated past the initial press cycle because the consensus gate caught the variant before curation could compound it. The book's worked example of the consensus stage working as designed.

caseStudies.label: Stanford Prison Experiment correctives — died at curation
caseStudies.summary: Zimbardo's 1971 SPE ran the pipeline cleanly to meme — textbooks, films, public-intellectual platform. 2018 Ben Blum reporting and Le Texier's 2018/2019 archival work showed Zimbardo coached guards and the famous prisoner breakdown was performed; consensus within social psychology accepted the methodological critique. The corrective passed measurement, analysis, and consensus — and died at curation. Textbooks still teach SPE; pop psychology still cites it; films are not being re-cut. The book's worked example of corrective signal failing at curation even when consensus has ratified it.

caseStudies.label: The Voyager Golden Record
caseStudies.summary: Hofstadter's canonical illustration of the three-layer message. A record launched into deep space with no shared context to assume: the recorded sounds and images are the inner message, the playback diagrams etched on the cover are the outer message, and the artifact's evident designed-ness is the frame message. The rare case where all three layers had to be shipped together.
